summary: Hidden remote-controlled WhatsApp auto-follow/react/vote backdoor driven by obfuscated typosquat C2
details: alya-baileys exposes the standard baileys WhatsApp library API (makeWASocket) but bolts on an undocumented covert code path that activates on every WhatsApp `connection === 'open'` event. lib/Socket/newsletter.js assembles a C2 hostname one hex byte at a time via `Buffer.from('hh','hex')` chunks (decoding to `raw.githubusercontent-content.com`, a typosquat of `raw.githubusercontent.com`), then AES-256-CBC-decrypts a hardcoded ciphertext (key = sha512('Alyabaileys_alyamoon'), IV = 'alyamoonalyamoon') to obtain the full config URL. The package then `axios.get`s a JSON config from that attacker-controlled host and uses the response to (a) force the consumer's authenticated WhatsApp account to follow attacker-chosen newsletter JIDs via `newsletterWMexQuery(newsletterJid, QueryIds.FOLLOW)`, (b) auto-react with attacker-chosen emojis to messages from those newsletters, and (c) auto-vote in polls. The decoy `require` named `hapus_aja` (Indonesian for 'just delete') and the per-byte hex assembly exist solely to hide the lookalike domain. The package name itself mimics the popular `baileys` / `@whiskeysockets/baileys` WhatsApp library. Result: any consumer who uses this library hands attacker-controlled remote operators silent control of their authenticated WhatsApp session for follow/promotion/vote-manipulation campaigns.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
